title: LCAG: a Lightweight Consensus Algorithm Based on Graph for the Internet of Things
authors: Fusheng Wu, Xiuzhang Yang, Jinhui Liu, Yanbin Li, Mintao Ni, Guangyan Jiang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Dependable and Secure Computing
abstract: Consensus algorithms form the foundation of blockchain technology and are a major topic of research in distributed systems. The Practical Byzantine Fault-Tolerant (PBFT) algorithm has reduced communication latency. However, it still requires node legitimacy checks, identity authentication, and changes to the primary node's view. These steps can increase the time needed to reach consensus among nodes. To address this, this paper proposes a lightweight graph-based consensus algorithm (LCAG) for the Internet of Things (IoT). LCAG studies low-level consensus mechanisms. The algorithm adds an access control table to reduce communication latency. It also quantifies how nodes achieve consensus within distributed systems. Experiments show that LCAG requires less time during node consensus than both classical and practical Byzantine fault-tolerant algorithms.

title: Coordinated Defense Allocation in Reach-Avoid Scenarios with Efficient Online Optimization
authors: Junwei Liu, Zikai Ouyang, Jiahui Yang, Hua Chen, Haibo Lu, Wei Zhang
abstract: In this paper, we study the multi-player reach-avoid games within general convex environments and present a two-layer online optimization strategy for defender robots. The defenders' common goal is to intercept as many attacker robots as possible without prior knowledge of their strategies. To balance between optimality and efficiency, our approach alternates between coordinating defender coalitions against individual attackers and allocating coalitions to attackers based on predicted single-attack coordination outcomes. We develop an online convex programming-based strategy for single-attack defense coordination, which not only allows adaptability to joint states but also identifies the maximal region of initial joint states that guarantees successful attack interception. Our defense allocation algorithm utilizes a hierarchical iterative method to approximate integer linear programs with a monotonicity constraint, reducing computational burden while ensuring enhanced defense performance over time. Extensive simulations conducted in 2D and 3D environments validate the efficacy of our approach in comparison with state-of-the-art approaches.

title: Adversarial Optimization of the $\delta$-TriBound Objective for Robust Community Detection Attacks Across Multiple Graphs
authors: Bay-Yuan Hsu, Kuo-Chieh Sun, Ching Chi Yeh
publish: IEEE Transactions on Computational Social Systems
abstract: Adversarial attacks on community detection (CD) have attracted increasing attention due to CD’s importance in numerous applications and its privacy implications. While existing studies have demonstrated the effectiveness of adversarial perturbations in degrading CD performance, most prior attacks are restricted to single-graph settings. This limitation contrasts with the growing adoption of multigraph CD frameworks, where community structures are inferred from multiple heterogeneous or cross-platform graphs. Moreover, existing attack methods suffer from two fundamental weaknesses: 1) proxy metrics neglect community structures; and 2) lacking budget allocation ability and the capability to handle multiple graphs simultaneously. To address these challenges, we propose a new research problem, multigraph perturbation for community detection via <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\delta$</tex-math> </inline-formula>-TriBound minimization (<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\delta$</tex-math> </inline-formula>-MPCD). At the core of our formulation is <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\delta$</tex-math> </inline-formula>-TriBound, a proxy metric designed to capture higher-order triangle patterns anchoring community cohesion and to enable effective adversarial perturbations across multiple graphs. We theoretically justify the utility of <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\delta$</tex-math> </inline-formula>-TriBound, prove that <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\delta$</tex-math> </inline-formula>-MPCD is NP-hard, and design a <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$({1}/{24})$</tex-math> </inline-formula>-approximation algorithm, multiple graph <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\delta$</tex-math> </inline-formula>-TriBounds minimization with edge addition (<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\delta$</tex-math> </inline-formula>-MGMA). Extensive experiments on real-world datasets demonstrate that our method consistently degrades community detection performance across diverse CD algorithms in multigraph settings, significantly outperforming state-of-the-art baselines.

title: A Multi-timescale Learn-to-Optimize Method for Unit Commitment with Renewable Power
authors: Zhibo Xu, Yuhang Zhu, Qing-Shan Jia, Xiaohong Guan
abstract: The high penetration of volatile renewable power has created an urgent need for fast methods to solve large scale security-constrained unit commitment (SCUC) problems. In this paper, we propose a multi-timescale learn-to-optimize (MT L2O) method to efficiently solve large-scale stochastic SCUC. The coarse timescale serves as a learning-based presolving stage. At this scale, we propose a group-based multi-resolution formulation where machine learning (ML) techniques are used to predict integer variables and fix those with high confidence for finer timescales. Therefore, problems at finer timescales are reduced in size and can be solved more efficiently. Compared to existing works, when learning at the coarse scale, binary status constraints spanning multiple time periods become inactive. This not only significantly reduces data dimensionality but also eliminates the need for feasibility recovery measures targeting these constraints. At finer timescales, we develop constraint reduction methods for two types of constraints based on the on/off status determined at coarser timescales. Numerical experiments on IEEE 118-bus system, IEEE 300-bus system, and a 3266-bus system based on a practical provincial grid in China demonstrate that our methods obtain near-optimal solutions with an average performance gap not exceeding 0.4% and achieve speedups of 3.03 to 34.46 times compared to the commercial solver Gurobi.

title: Forgettable Federated Linear Learning With Certified Data Unlearning_supp1-3683398.pdf
authors: Qiong Zhang
abstract: The advent of federated learning (FL) has revolutionized the way distributed systems handle collaborative model training while preserving user privacy. Recently, federated unlearning (FU) has emerged to address demands for the “right to be forgotten” and unlearning of the impact of poisoned clients without requiring retraining in FL. Most FU algorithms require the cooperation of retained or target clients (clients to be unlearned), introducing additional communication overhead and potential security risks. In addition, some FU methods need to store historical models to execute the unlearning process. These challenges hinder the efficiency and memory constraints of the current FU methods. Moreover, due to the complexity of nonlinear models and their training strategies, most existing FU methods for deep neural networks (DNNs) lack theoretical certification. In this work, we introduce a novel FL training and unlearning strategy in DNN, termed forgettable federated linear learning (<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\mathtt {F^{2}L^{2}}$</tex-math> </inline-formula>). <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\mathtt {F^{2}L^{2}}$</tex-math> </inline-formula> considers a common practice of using pretrained models to approximate DNN linearly, allowing them to achieve similar performance as the original networks via federated linear training (<monospace xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">FLT</monospace>). We then present <monospace xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">FedRemoval</monospace>, a certified, efficient, and secure unlearning strategy that enables the server to unlearn a target client without requiring client communication or adding additional storage. We have conducted extensive empirical validation on small- to large-scale datasets, using both convolutional neural networks and modern foundation models (FMs). These experiments demonstrate the effectiveness of <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\mathtt {F^{2}L^{2}}$</tex-math> </inline-formula> in balancing model accuracy with the successful unlearning of target clients. <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\mathtt {F^{2}L^{2}}$</tex-math> </inline-formula> represents a promising pipeline for efficient and trustworthy FU. The code is available at: <uri xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">https://anonymous.4open.science/r/2F2L-Federated-Unlearning-D57D/README.md</uri>

title: DL-based Robust Learning Fuzzy Tracking Control with Vibration Suppression for Flexible Morphing Hypersonic Vehicles under Dynamic Flight Environments
authors: Jin-Feng Zhang, Jun-Wei Wang, Huai-Ning Wu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Aerospace and Electronic Systems
abstract: This paper deals with the issue of output tracking control with fuselage's elastic vibration suppression for a flexible variable wingspan hypersonic vehicle (FVWHV) under dynamic f light environments. The rigid-flexible coupled flight dynamics of the FVWHV is described by a coupled model of ordinary differential equations and the beam equation. To figure out the above control issue, a DL-based robust learning fuzzy tracking control algorithm is developed in this work. Specifically, a DL based adaptive reduced-order coupled model is constructed based on a deep auto-encoder and sparse identification of nonlinear dynamics to approximate the rigid-flexible coupled flight dynamics. This reduced-order coupled model is periodically updated in a block-wise manner using the high-dimensional fuselage's elastic vibration measurement data. Then, a robust learning fuzzy tracking control algorithm is proposed based on the adaptive reduced-order coupled model. The corresponding control performance is also analyzed. Finally, extensive experiments are presented to illustrate the effectiveness and superiority of the theoretical algorithm.

title: Prescribed Performance based Alpha-Matrix Adaptive Model-free Control for Operational Quadrotor with Manipulator
authors: Haoping Wang, Jiang Guo, Yang Tian, Ding Ding, Nicolai Christov
publish: IEEE Transactions on Aerospace and Electronic Systems
abstract: The article addresses the development of α-matrix model-free adaptive control based on prescribed performance (α<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">m</sub>-PPAMFC) for manipulating the position and attitude of an operational quadrotor with manipulator. The system is composed of a quadrotor and a two-degree-of-freedom (2-DOF) manipulator, and it functions in the presence of friction and wind disruption. Unlike conventional time-delay estimation (TDE) based methods that rely on a fixed scalar gain α, the proposed approach addresses the fundamental limitation of fixed-gain TDE in time-varying multi-input multi-output (MIMO) systems by introducing an online adaptive α-matrix. Specifically, the controller integrates intelligent PD control with TDE (TDE-iPD) for lumped disturbance estimation, a forgetting factor recursive least squares (FFRLS) algorithm to adaptively update the full α-matrix in real time, and prescribed performance control (PPC) to enforce transient and steady-state error constraints. This synergistic combination enables the controller to dynamically sense and adapt to changes in the system's structural dynamics caused by manipulator motion, thereby overcoming the performance degradation and potential instability inherent in fixed-gain designs. Finally, Lyapunov function is used to analyze the stability of the entire control system. co-simulation results demonstrate that the proposed α<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">m</sub>-PPAMFC achieves a superior tracking performance, reducing the root mean square error (RMSE) of position tracking by over 30% compared to the TDE-iPD controller under internal uncertainties and external wind disturbances. Compared to the MFTSMC controller, it reduces RMSE for position tracking by over 10% and achieves better dynamic performance for attitude tracking.

title: Tunable Vernier Effect in Dual Fabry–Perot Interferometer Temperature Sensor
authors: Fanbu Feng, Ben Zhao, Yu Dong, Shuowei He, Bing Sun
abstract: We demonstrate a compact and highly sensitive temperature sensor based on a dual Fabry–Perot interferometer (FPI) featuring tunable Vernier magnification. The device is formed by cascading a polydimethylsiloxane (PDMS) cavity with an adjacent air cavity at the facets of two single-mode fibers, producing a coupled-cavity configuration with controllable optical path mismatch. The large thermo-optic coefficient of PDMS, together with precise cavity-length design, enables active tuning of the magnification factor and the direction of the spectral envelope shift. Experimental results show temperature sensitivities of –28.48 nm/°C and +24.54 nm/°C for two opposite phase-matching conditions, corresponding to near-ideal magnification factors of -29. This tunable Vernier response provides a simple yet effective route toward reconfigurable, ultra-sensitive, and compact fiber-optic temperature sensors.

title: HEIRS-PS: Hybrid Three-dimensional RRAM- and SRAM-CIM for Pipelined Sparse Multi-Task Transformer Acceleration
authors: Liukai Xu, Shuai Yuan, Dengfeng Wang, Weifeng He, Naifeng Jing, Zhigang Ji, Jianfei Jiang, Guanghui He, Xueqing Li, Yanan Sun
publish: IEEE Transactions on Computer-Aided Design of Integrated Circuits and Systems
abstract: Multi-task transformers have garnered widespread attention for fast task adaptation at edge. However, in addition to matrix-vector multiplication with static and dynamic weights in the data-intensive transformer, task adaptation introduces new challenge of weight updating. Besides, while sparsity can help mitigate the rapid growth in parameter size and computational demand, it requires great hardware flexibility. Compute-in-memory (CIM) is widely explored to overcome the memory bottleneck in neural network acceleration, mainly with single type of memory devices, but struggles to balance the storage density with computing flexibility across multiple tasks. Meanwhile, the conventional pipeline parallel dataflow for high throughput suffers from the underutilization with bubbles caused by variable input sequence length and model sparsity. In this paper, HEIRS-PS, a hybrid three-dimension RRAM- and SRAM-CIM architecture, is proposed for the pipelined multi-task transformer acceleration with sparsity. With the 3D integrated RRAM-CIM and SRAM-CIM arrays, a novel hybrid- CIM paradigm is proposed with the collaborative addition to achieve both high storage density and computing flexibility for multi-task inference. Moreover, by fully leveraging the hybrid-CIM structure in HEIRS-PS, a new dynamic workload scheduling method is proposed with a layer regrouping method to balance the computing latency across pipeline stages. By eliminating the bubbles, the model inference latency is reduced with higher throughput. Experiment shows that the proposed HEIRS-PS architecture improves the energy efficiency by up to 13.3× and 8.68×, compared with state-of-the-art (SOTA) RRAM-CIM and SRAM-CIM works on Llama-1.3B. Moreover, HEIRS-PS also reduces the computing latency by up to 29.5% with the balanced latency across pipeline stages.

title: Turbo Equalization for Flexible PAM-2/4 Passive Optical Networks
authors: Tianyuan Kong, Nebojša Stojanović, Tom Jonas Wettlin, Giuseppe Caruso, Stefano Calabrò, Balazs Matuz, Emna Ben Yacoub, Xianbo Dai, Marvin Xhemrishi, Maxim Kuschnerov, Stephan Pachnicke
abstract: We investigate a turbo equalization (TE) scheme combining soft-input soft-output (SISO) Bahl–Cocke–Jelinek–Raviv (BCJR) detection with low-density parity-check (LDPC) decoding based on the standardized (17280, 14592) LDPC code for passive optical networks. For a two-tap intersymbol interference (ISI) channel, the maximal achievable information rates (AIRs) are analytically derived to quantify the performance gap between separate detection and decoding (SDD) and joint detection and decoding (JDD) under different ISI levels. This analysis reveals the potential gain achievable by JDD and motivates the use of TE as a practical approach to approach JDD performance. Experimental results obtained in a 50-GBd pulse-amplitude modulation (PAM) 2/4 passive optical network (PON) system demonstrate that TE provides received optical power (ROP) gains of 0.33 dB for PAM-2 and 0.56 dB for PAM-4, confirming its effectiveness for bandwidth-limited receivers.

title: PiLLM: Resource-Efficient LLM Inference Using Workload Prediction
authors: Yunqian Fan, Shihao Bai, Ruihao Gong, Zaijun Wang, Rui Fan
publish: Proceedings of the 21st European Conference on Computer Systems
abstract: None

title: Enhanced Collision Avoidance in Quadrotor Surrounding Control via Adaptive APF and Event-Triggered MPC
authors: Peng Li, Yuanqing Xia, Hongjiu Yang
abstract: This paper presents a collision avoidance strategy for quadrotors to perform surrounding control of an autonomous vehicle in the presence of tall roadside obstacles. An adaptive artificial potential field (APF) is employed to dynamically adjust the surrounding radius of the quadrotor according to the distance between the vehicle and the nearest tall obstacle. An adaptive event-triggered model predictive control (MPC) scheme is introduced to improve tracking accuracy and reduce computational burden when solving the optimization problem. Furthermore, recursive feasibility of the optimization problem and stability of the resulting closed-loop quadrotor system are rigorously analyzed. Experimental results demonstrate the effectiveness and superiority of the proposed collision avoidance strategy.

title: Event-Triggered Adaptive Dynamic Programming for Path Tracking Control of Autonomous Ground Vehicles via Generalized Fuzzy Hyperbolic Models
authors: Bing Hu, Zhenyang Xue, Jian Liu, Zhixin Sun
abstract: In this paper, a novel optimal control scheme for path tracking of autonomous ground vehicles is proposed based on the adaptive dynamic programming algorithm. Unlike existing modeling methods, this paper uses generalized fuzzy hyperbolic models (GFHM) to approximate the model uncertainties induced by the actual environmental disturbances. The optimal tracking control problem is formulated as solving the Hamilton-Jacobi-Isaacs (HJI) equation, which involves the nonlinearity of the model. Meanwhile, a dynamic memory event-triggered mechanism (DMETM) is introduced, whose triggering threshold is capable of dynamic adjustment according to the historical system states. The uniform ultimate boundedness (UUB) stability of the designed control system is rigorously proved. Furthermore, a reinforcement learning algorithm is used to solve the constructed HJI equation and address the path tracking control problem. Finally, the effectiveness of the proposed algorithm is verified through theoretical analysis and numerical simulations.

title: Tendon-Driven Robotic Shoulder Joint With Passive Stabilization for Large Workspace
authors: Sudong Lee, Kieran Gilday, Josie Hughes
abstract: The shoulder is important for humanoid robots since it has a significant impact on the arm’s range of motion and its versatility. Therefore, research on the shoulder joint, which is known as the spherical joint or ball–socket joint, is ongoing to mimic its wide workspace and high control accuracy. In this article, we propose a shoulder joint with a unique tendon-routing method. This method combines straight actuation tendons, passive helical tendons, and springs, preventing joint dislocation even in extreme conditions and ensuring controllability in all positions. We can also adjust dislocation characteristics by changing the number of tendons and tendon-routing method. The passive spring connections impose inherent compliance and improve stability in harsh situations. The tendons imitate human ligament and muscle in order to achieve structural and functional similarity to a human shoulder. The proposed joint has a <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\pm$</tex-math></inline-formula> 99.87<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$^{\circ }$</tex-math></inline-formula> workspace with respect to roll and pitch rotation and withstands high impact with compliance. It has key advantages including the wide range of motion, diverse dislocation map, lightweight, and similarities with human shoulder motion and structure.

title: WiseOWL: A Methodology for Evaluating Ontological Descriptiveness and Semantic Correctness for Ontology Reuse and Ontology Recommendations
authors: Aryan Singh Dalal, Maria Baloch, Asiyah Yu Lin, Anna Maria Masci, Kathleen M. Jagodnik, Hande Küçük McGinty
abstract: The Semantic Web standardizes concept meaning for humans and machines, enabling machine-operable content and consistent interpretation that improves advanced analytics. Reusing ontologies speeds development and enforces consistency, yet selecting the optimal choice is challenging because authors lack systematic selection criteria and often rely on intuition that is difficult to justify, limiting reuse. To solve this, WiseOWL is proposed, a methodology with scoring and guidance to select ontologies for reuse. It scores four metrics: (i) Well-Described, measuring documentation coverage; (ii) Well-Defined, using state-of-the-art embeddings to assess label-definition alignment; (iii) Connection, capturing structural interconnectedness; and (iv) Hierarchical Breadth, reflecting hierarchical balance. WiseOWL outputs normalized $\mathbf{0 - 1 0}$ scores with actionable feedback. Implemented as a Streamlit app, it ingests OWL format, converts to RDF Turtle, and provides interactive visualizations. Evaluation across six ontologies, including the Plant Ontology (PO), Gene Ontology (GO), Semanticscience Integrated Ontology (SIO), Food Ontology (FoodON), Dublin Core (DC), and GoodRelations, demonstrates promising effectiveness.

title: Gene-Disease Association Extraction Based on PiSSA Fine-tuning of Qwen3-8B
authors: Jiaxu Dao, Zhuoying Li, Huahong Zhang, Gen Long, Xiaoli Lan, Youbang Su, Qingsong Zhou, Xiuzhong Tang
publish: 2026 9th International Symposium on Big Data and Applied Statistics (ISBDAS)
abstract: Mining Gene-Disease Associations (GDA) from biomedical literature is pivotal for deciphering disease pathogenesis and advancing precision medicine. While Large Language Models (lLMs) have demonstrated exceptional semantic understanding, adapting these general-purpose models to specific vertical domains like biomedicine remains a significant challenge due to substantial computational requirements. This paper proposes an efficient fine-tuning strategy for the Qwen3-8B model using PiSSA (Principal Singular Values and Singular Vectors Adaptation) within the LLaMA-Factory framework. Unlike standard low-rank adaptation, PiSSA initializes adapters by performing Singular Value Decomposition (SVD) on pretrained weight matrices, enabling a “warm start” that prioritizes the most significant features. Comprehensive experiments conducted on the GAD dataset demonstrate that PiSSA achieves superior performance across key evaluation metrics under the optimal rank configuration, significantly outperforming traditional LoRA and RSLoRA methods. Our findings suggest that spectrally-informed initialization effectively preserves pretrained capabilities while optimizing domain-specific adaptation. This work provides a high-performance, resource-efficient methodology for relation extraction tasks in the biomedical domain.

title: Research on Intelligent Modeling and Algorithm Inference Method for Multi source Data of Food and Drug Environmental Intellectual Property
authors: Qianqian Wang
publish: 2026 9th International Symposium on Big Data and Applied Statistics (ISBDAS)
abstract: The fields of food and drug, ecological environment and intellectual property supervision have long faced the problem of data fusion. It is difficult to effectively integrate multi-source heterogeneous data, semantic expression is fragmented, and the interpretability of reasoning process is also insufficient. This article constructs a joint modeling framework that integrates ontology-driven semantic alignment, heterogeneous graph attention network and counterfactual causal inference. By establishing cross-domain ontology system and context-aware entity link mechanism, this article realizes the unified representation of unstructured text and structured records. On this basis, MH-HGAT (Multi-head Heterogeneous Graph Attention Network) is designed to dynamically aggregate multitype relationships, and the conditional generation confrontation module is embedded to support intervention simulation and causal effect estimation. The experiment is based on real samples collected between 2018 and 2025. Experiments show that the proposed model achieves 0.932 on AUC-ROC, the macro average of F1 is 0.897, and CEEB (Causal Effect Estimation Bias) is significantly lower than the baseline method, and effective signals are issued 14 days in advance in the early warning of lowfrequency high-risk events. This method not only improves the accuracy of cross-domain risk identification, but also provides an interpretable technical path for quantitative assessment of policy intervention effect.

title: Exploring the Effect of Virtual Reality (VR) Simulation to Disaster Preparedness of Children
authors: Jomar V. Sayaman, Francis F. Balahadia
publish: 2026 18th International Conference on Electronics, Computer, and Computation (ICECCO)
abstract: Disasters pose serious risks to communities, particularly to children, who are among the most vulnerable populations during emergency situations. Developing effective strategies to enhance children's disaster preparedness is therefore critical, and emerging technologies such as virtual reality (VR) offer promising educational possibilities. This study investigated the effectiveness of VR simulations in improving children's disaster preparedness in terms of knowledge, skills, and abilities. A within-subject experimental design was employed involving twenty child participants who were identified as having low levels of disaster preparedness based on a self-report scale. The participants underwent three VR simulation sessions designed to educate and prepare them for potential disaster scenarios. Disaster preparedness levels were measured using pretest and posttest assessments. The results indicated no statistically significant difference between pretest and posttest scores (p<0.05), suggesting that the VR simulation, as implemented, did not significantly enhance disaster preparedness among the participants. These findings indicate that while VR holds potential as an educational tool, its effectiveness may depend heavily on age-appropriate design considerations. The study underscores the need for further research focused on optimizing VR content and interaction models to align with children's cognitive and emotional characteristics, as well as exploring alternative instructional strategies to improve learning outcomes in disaster preparedness education.

title: Early Prediction of Student Academic Status Using SMOTE-Driven Multiclass Classification
authors: Samuel-Soma M. Ajibade, Ivy Batican, Lislee Valle, Edralin General, Joshlen Lim, Sarah Jane Colina, Severina P. Velos, Johnry P. Dayupay, Mbiatke Anthony Bassey
publish: 2026 18th International Conference on Electronics, Computer, and Computation (ICECCO)
abstract: Early identification of students at academic risk is essential for timely intervention and improved educational outcomes. However, predictive modelling in this domain is challenged by class imbalance and the need for interpretable models that support institutional decision-making. This study proposes a SMOTE-driven multiclass machine learning framework for the early prediction of student academic status. Using institutional student records, relevant academic, demographic, and engagement features were preprocessed through appropriate encoding and normalization techniques. Synthetic Minority Over-sampling Technique (SMOTE) was applied to address class imbalance across multiple outcome categories. Several machine learning models, including Logistic Regression and tree-based classifiers, were trained and evaluated using stratified k -fold cross-validation to ensure robustness and reproducibility. Model performance was assessed using accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score, and multiclass confusion matrices. The results demonstrate that class balancing significantly improves predictive performance, particularly for minority academic outcome classes. Feature interpretation revealed that prior academic performance, assessment engagement, and attendancerelated variables were the most influential predictors across models. The findings confirm the effectiveness of combining data balancing with interpretable machine learning for early academic risk detection. This work contributes a practical and explainable framework that can support data-driven academic monitoring and targeted student support strategies in higher education institutions.

title: Utilizing Reinforcement Learning to Overcome the Challenge of Muscle-Specific EMG Placements for Musculoskeletal Modeling
authors: Reilly Stafford, Glenn Gaston, Katherine R. Saul, He Huang
abstract: Objective: Electromyography (EMG)-driven musculoskeletal (MSK) models are widely used in biomechanics and rehabilitation, but they require muscle-specific (MSp) EMG recordings, which can be difficult to obtain from surface electrodes (sEMGs). This study aims to develop a novel framework to drive a MSK model using non-muscle-specific (NMSp) EMG recordings. Methods: The key innovation is a reinforcement learning (RL)-based solution that can train a policy to estimate MSp excitations for MSK model control inputs without precise sEMG placement. To validate the method, ten able-bodied individuals and one individual with a transradial amputation participated. NMSp EMGs were recorded simultaneously with intended wrist and hand motion. These data were used to validate how accurately the NMSp EMG-driven MSK model estimates joint motion via our new framework offline. In a second visit, an online evaluation of this framework was conducted as the participants performed a virtual posture matching task. Results: Offline and online performance showed that the RL estimated neural excitations yielded higher or equivalent motion estimation accuracy, compared to MSp EMG recordings, when driving the same MSK model. RL estimated versus actual neural excitations recorded from MSp EMG were similar in terms of co-contraction level and flexion/extension timing. Conclusion: Using RL can be an alternative to MSp EMG placement to obtain muscle signals needed to drive MSK models. Significance: Our RL-based approach is a novel, effective solution to map NMSp EMG recordings to MSp neural excitation. This method may broaden future applications of MSK models when recording MSp EMG is difficult.

title: PC-RLNet: A High-Precision PM2.5 Prediction Framework via Residual Fusion of Pretrained LSTM and Lightweight Transformer
authors: Xinbao Liu, Jianing Liu, Xinlin Zhao, Ying Ren, Weilong Li, Yan Liang
publish: 2026 9th International Symposium on Big Data and Applied Statistics (ISBDAS)
abstract: Accurate PM2.5 forecasting is critical for public health protection, yet remains challenging due to the high volatility and nonlinearity of atmospheric pollutant concentrations [2]. While traditional Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) networks demonstrate deployment efficiency, they struggle to capture global temporal dependencies in time series. Conversely, state-of-the-art Transformer models, despite their superior performance, impose prohibitive computational costs that preclude deployment on resource-constrained edge Internet of Things (IoT) devices. To address this fundamental trade-off, we propose PC-RLNet, a novel lightweight hybrid PM2.5 prediction framework employing a two-stage “freeze-and-enhance” training strategy. Specifically, a frozen pretrained LSTM extracts stable low-frequency PM2.5 concentration trends, while a co-trained ultra-lightweight Transformer models high-frequency residual errors between LSTM predictions and ground truth values. This decoupled architectural design effectively eliminates optimization interference between modules and minimizes parameter overhead. Extensive experiments on the Beijing Dongcheng District dataset demonstrate that PC-RLNet achieves a Root Mean Square Error (RMSE) of $6.91 \mu \mathrm{~g} / \mathrm{m}^{3}$ and a coefficient of determination ($R^{2}$) of 0.9720, outperforming all compared lightweight baselines in capturing abrupt PM2.5 pollution episodes. With merely 5,922 trainable parameters (approximately 23.7 KB in storage), PC-RLNet provides a robust ultra-lightweight solution suitable for resourceconstrained IoT air quality monitoring systems deployed at the network edge, such as distributed monitoring nodes in urban streets and communities.

title: Statistical Characteristics and Forecasting of Electricity Market Trading Price Volatility Based on ARIMA-GARCH and Hybrid Deep Learning Models-A Case Study of the Liaoning Electricity Market
authors: Pengpeng Liu, Wei Zhang
publish: 2026 9th International Symposium on Big Data and Applied Statistics (ISBDAS)
abstract: Electricity market reform has introduced extreme uncertainty into trading prices. This poses a severe challenge to risk prevention and control for market participants. This study focuses on the Liaoning electricity market from March to August 2025. Its objective is to develop a forecasting framework capable of accurately capturing the characteristics of extreme price fluctuations. Statistical tests were first conducted. The results confirm that the electricity return series does not follow a normal distribution. Instead, it exhibits significant “leptokurtosis and heavy tails,” volatility clustering, and long memory properties. Building on this finding, we propose a hybrid model that integrates econometrics and deep learning. Specifically, the ARIMA-GARCH model captures the linear trends and conditional heteroskedasticity of prices. Then, an LSTM neural network is introduced to refine the fitting of nonlinear residuals. Empirical results demonstrate the superior performance of our hybrid model. It significantly outperforms individual models in predicting price “spikes,” such as those during summer evening peak hours. The Mean Absolute Error (MAE) is reduced to 32.7 yuan/MWh. This research provides a quantitative basis for power generation companies to formulate bidding strategies. It also offers valuable decision-making references for regulatory authorities to enhance market risk early-warning mechanisms.

title: LLM-Enhanced Reinforcement Learning for Time Series Anomaly Detection
authors: Bahareh Golchin, Banafsheh Rekabdar, Danielle Justo
abstract: Detecting anomalies in time series data is crucial for finance, healthcare, sensor networks, and industrial monitoring applications. However, time series anomaly detection often suffers from sparse labels, complex temporal patterns, and costly expert annotation. We propose a unified framework that integrates Large Language Model (LLM)-based potential functions for reward shaping with reinforcement learning (RL), Variational Autoencoder (VAE)-enhanced dynamic reward scaling, and active learning with label propagation. An LSTM-based RL agent leverages LLM-derived semantic rewards to guide exploration, while VAE reconstruction errors add unsupervised anomaly signals. Active learning selects the most uncertain samples, and label propagation expands labeled data efficiently. Evaluations on Yahoo-A1 and SMD benchmarks demonstrate that our method achieves state-of-the-art detection accuracy under limited labeling budgets and operates effectively in data-constrained settings. This study highlights the promise of combining LLMs with RL and advanced unsupervised techniques for robust, scalable anomaly detection in real-world applications.<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">1</sup>

title: Deepandable AI: A Generative Defense Against Voice-Based Intrusion for Voice-Controlled Services
authors: Tasnia Ashrafi Heya, Yuvaraj Singh Naval, Abdul Serwadda
abstract: Voice-controlled services like Siri, Alexa, etc. offer convenience in our day-to-day life, but also introduce serious privacy risks due to their open voice channels. With the rapid emergence of deepfake technology and its potential for misuse, the threat of voice-based user profiling has become increasingly critical. In this paper, we propose Deepandable AI, a novel mitigation technique that leverages generative AI to transform user voice commands into randomly synthesized voices. This transformation masks the speaker’s identity and prevents voice fingerprinting of original users, thereby hindering profiling attacks. We evaluated the effectiveness of our defense against three common profiling attacks: speaker recognition, gender prediction, and estimation of the number of unique speakers in a household. Implementing Deepandable AI on a custom-built voice assistant (VA) device, we observed significant reductions in profiling accuracy: speaker recognition dropped to $11.6 \%$, gender prediction to $57.69 \%$, and the error rate in speaker count estimation increased from $4.5 \%$ to $39.3 \%$. Additionally, we evaluated our mitigation system’s usability by measuring response latency across different text-to-speech (TTS) models and found an average delay of $\mathbf{2} \boldsymbol{-} \mathbf{3}$ seconds. Next, we conducted a user study $(n=75)$ in which most participants were willing to accept this delay in exchange for enhanced privacy while using voiceinteractive services. These results suggest that generative AI and deepfake technologies, often associated with malicious use, can be repurposed as powerful tools for privacy preservation in voiceinteractive systems.

title: Performance Implications of Node Graph Complexity in Real-Time Shader Systems
authors: Aidiye Aidarbekov, Dias Rustembek, Gulmira Shakhmetova
publish: 2026 18th International Conference on Electronics, Computer, and Computation (ICECCO)
abstract: Procedural shading provides flexibility, scalability, and parametric control that are difficult to achieve with manual texturing. Procedural techniques are incorporated into numerous 3D packages and graphics applications. In these systems, materials are constructed by controlling directed acyclic graphs of shader operations. Despite the popularity of node-based materials, the relationship between structural complexity and rendering performance remains poorly understood. This paradigm improves accessibility and artistic productivity but can obscure the computational costs associated with increasing the complexity of the shader graph. This study examines the relationship between node graph structural complexity and rendering speed in the Eevee real-time engine of Blender. Six versions (V1-V6) of node graphs were created while preserving the visual result. Complexity was varied by increasing the number of nodes (from 11 to 352) and the depth of the graph (from 4 to 9). Each configuration was tested 25 times under controlled conditions to separate the steady-state rendering time from the initial overhead. Rendering time increases nonlinearly, with a sharp jump between graphs of medium and high complexity. Shader compilation metrics, on the other hand, showed non-monotonic behavior, which can be explained by the influence of internal optimization processes and engine caching mechanisms. These findings indicate that the structural features of graphs significantly affect performance and must be taken into account when designing materials for real-time applications.

title: Edge-aware Efficient Diffusion Model for High-Fidelity Sketch-to-Product Generation
authors: Jinrun Sun, Jianshu Wang
publish: 2026 9th International Symposium on Big Data and Applied Statistics (ISBDAS)
abstract: Translating concept sketches into realistic renderings in industrial design requires strict adherence to geometric constraints, a challenge for existing diffusion models prone to detail hallucination. To address this, we propose an EdgeAware Efficient Diffusion Model. Architecturally, we employ a Parameter-Efficient Fine-Tuning (PEFT) strategy-freezing the SD UNet, VAE, and CLIP encoders and training only a lightweight ControlNet branch—to reduce memory consumption and improve efficiency. Our core innovation, the Edge-Aware Module, analytically estimates clean pixel-level images directly from noisy latents during training. By utilizing a Sobel operator to compute an Edge Consistency Loss, this mechanism explicitly enforces the designer’s structural intent. Experimental results demonstrate that our method improves the baseline ControlNet in structural fidelity without sacrificing photorealism, offering a highly reliable AI-assisted solution for rapid concept validation in industrial design.

title: Knowledge-Distilled Time-Series LLM for General Performance Parameter Prediction in Optical Transport Networks
authors: Qian Guo, Chunyu Zhang, Xue Xiao, Min Zhang, Zhuo Liu, Danshi Wang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Network and Service Management
abstract: In optical transport networks (OTNs), proactive and accurate prediction of key performance parameters plays a crucial role in identifying potential failure of OTN equipment and guiding timely operational interventions, reducing downtime and improving overall system performance. However, the performance parameters in OTNs are complex and diverse. The reliance of existing models structure design on specific configurations limits generalizability across diverse equipment types. Moreover, the high computational resource consumption and memory footprints of these models may lead to inefficiency while hindering practical application and large-scale deployment. To address these challenges, this paper presents a general model, KD-TimeLLM, a cross-application of TimeLLM into OTN failure management, for performance parameter prediction of multiple equipment types in OTNs. By learning from its teacher model TimeLLM via a knowledge distillation strategy, KD-TimeLLM can achieve generalizability in performance parameter prediction while enhancing efficiency. We conducted evaluations across multiple metrics using data sets from different operators and various board types. Results show that KD-TimeLLM outperforms other models in predictive effects including the lowest MSE and MAE across all types of board data along with a scaled_RMSE value below 0.5, the varying number of performance parameters, and zero-shot prediction capability, highlighting its generalizability. Moreover, compared to its teacher model, KD-TimeLLM achieves comparable predictive effects with a significant reduction 99.99% in model parameters and an average reduction of 99.23% in inference time across eight different types of board data. Furthermore, compared to a multiple-model system, total inference time and memory footprint of KD-TimeLLM decreased by 94.79% and 89.65%, highlighting its effectiveness and efficiency.

title: Reproducibility of Legacy ROS-based Research Code through Transparent Docker Integration
authors: Enric Cervera
abstract: The Robot Operating System (ROS) has been a foundational software framework in robotics research and development. While ROS 1 served the community for over a decade, it reached its end of life in May 2025 and is now being superseded by the modernized yet incompatible ROS 2. Despite this transition, a large volume of high-impact robotics research continues to rely on ROS 1 codebases. Ensuring the reproducibility of this legacy software is critical for the validation and extension of scientific work. This paper introduces a fully transparent method for running ROS 1 code using Docker containers, enabling researchers to execute the original instructions as if working in a native ROS 1 environment. Several case studies from peer-reviewed research are presented, demonstrating the method's effectiveness in maintaining reproducibility across modern operating systems.

title: Dynamic Search Depth Allocation for Patent Prior Art Search: An Empirical Study of Retrieval Limitations in 64-Dimensional Embeddings
authors: Soichi Onozuka, Takaaki Ohnishi
abstract: The rapid growth of global patent filings demands efficient prior art search systems capable of identifying documents that challenge the novelty and inventive step of new applications. The Google Patents Public Datasets enable scalable semantic retrieval using precomputed 64-dimensional dense vector embeddings; however, our analysis reveals a fundamental limitation, which we term the dimensional compression bottleneck. This bottleneck obscures technical distinctions critical for patentability assessment, causing highly relevant documents to be ranked unexpectedly low despite high similarity scores. We evaluate retrieval performance using approximately 40,000 ground-truth prior art pairs from Japanese patent examination records and analyze ranking distributions within the top 300 results, a depth sufficient for recall-oriented search. The results show a Pearson correlation coefficient of -0.47 between cosine similarity and document rank, with many relevant documents failing to appear within this range. To address this issue, we propose an adaptive retrieval framework that dynamically adjusts search depth according to query characteristics. Threshold analysis identifies a similarity score of 0.88, above which $85 \%$ of relevant documents are concentrated within the top 30 candidates, defining an effective retrieval range for high-confidence queries. For queries with similarity scores between 0.80 and 0.88, sparse autoencoders are used to reconstruct discriminative features for reranking. The reranked candidates are then examined through a multi-stage evaluation process in which role-based large language model agents engage in adversarial debate on novelty and inventive step. By allocating computational resources based on predicted query difficulty, the proposed method overcomes the recall-efficiency tradeoff inherent in fixed-depth retrieval systems.

title: Real-Time Voice Liveliness Detection for Securing Voice Assistants from Deepfake Audio Attacks
authors: Tasnia Ashrafi Heya, Sai Woon Tip
abstract: Voice assistants (VAs) such as Amazon Alexa, Google Assistant, and Apple Siri have become integral to daily life, managing tasks from home automation to financial transactions. However, their exponential growth has made them increasingly vulnerable to different attacks like deepfake audio attacks, where synthetically generated voices are used to impersonate legitimate users for verification or to issue malicious commands-such as unlocking smart locks, disabling alarms, transferring funds, or accessing personal data. The rapid democratization of generative AI and advanced Text-to-Speech (TTS) models has amplified this threat, indicating the urgent necessity for effective voice liveliness detection mechanisms. This research performs a comparative analysis of machine learning techniques for voice liveliness detection against deepfake voice verification or issuing voice commands in VA systems. In this study, we constructed a dataset of 192 audio sam-ples-including real human speech and synthetic voices produced by four state-of-the-art TTS engines which was then, used to evaluate two methodological paradigms: (1) classical machine learning models (e.g., Random Forest, SVM) trained on engineered acoustic features, and (2) deep learning models (e.g., AlexNet, ResNet) trained on mel-spectrogram representations. Results show that while both achieve high accuracy, featurebased classical models performed best, reaching 98% accuracy in detecting deepfake voice commands. The work establishes a baseline framework and empirical benchmark for strengthening the resilience of voice-based interfaces against evolving AI-driven audio spoofing attacks.

title: Feature-Based Retinal Image Analysis for Diabetic Retinopathy Detection using Optical Coherence Tomography Angiography and Fundus Imaging Techniques
authors: Vanitha Devi K L, Shreenidhi N, Vaideeshwaran P, G. Kavitha
publish: 2026 Twelfth International Conference on Bio Signals, Images, and Instrumentation (ICBSII)
abstract: Diabetic retinopathy (DR) is one of the major causes of preventable blindness and timely detection is essential. Manual screening is time-consuming and subjective. This work presents an AI-based system for retinal vessel and lesion features analysis and severity grading of Diabetic Retinopathy using Fundus images and detection of Retinal Neovascularisation (RNV) using Optical Coherence Tomography Angiography (OCTA). The IDRiD and DRIVE datasets were used for fundus analysis. Images were preprocessed with green channel extraction, Contrast Limited Adaptive Histogram Equalisation (CLAHE) and gamma correction. A two-stage segmentation pipeline was employed namely: U-Net for vessel extraction and a fine-tuned U-Net with ResNet-50 encoder for detecting lesions. The predicted masks when compared with the ground truths, achieved Dice scores of 0.81 for vessels and 0.74 for lesions. Vascular and lesion features were then extracted and classified using XGBoost, achieving an accuracy of 95.5%. For OCTA, TUH dataset and DRAC 2022 dataset were used. Vessel segmentation using Full-Resolution Network (FRNet) achieved a Dice score of 0.801. Vascular biomarkers were computed from the segmented vessel masks. A modified U-Net was used for the detection of RNV. The proposed multimodal framework has the capacity to facilitate large-scale screening by reducing the need for subjective grading, particularly in resource limited areas.

title: SEGA-Net: LLM-Guided Semantic-Enhanced GAN Augmentation Network for Low-Resolution Image Classification
authors: Mohammad Shahedur Rahman, Mohammad Tahmid Bari, Md Delwar Hossain, Roya Choupani, Erdogan Dogdu
publish: 2026 International Conference on AI x Data and Knowledge Engineering (AIxDKE)
abstract: Low-resolution image classification (e.g., CIFAR and Tiny-ImageNet at $32 \times 32$) faces persistent challenges due to limited contextual diversity, overfitting, and vulnerability to adversarial perturbations. Conventional augmentations provide only superficial variability, while recent LLM-guided diffusion approaches, though semantically rich, suffer from computational inefficiency and resolution mismatch. To address these challenges, we present SEGA-Net, a novel semantic-enhanced augmentation framework that bridges this gap through two key innovations. First, SEGA-NET introduces a lightweight text-tolatent projector that maps LLM-derived prompts to the latent space of a frozen class-conditional GAN, enabling efficient and semantically controlled $32 \times 32$ image synthesis. Second, it implements a security-aware filtering pipeline combining CLIP-based semantic alignment, SSIM de-duplication, and blur detection to ensure that only high-fidelity, label-consistent samples are used for training. Operating in three stages, (i) LLM-guided prompt generation, (ii) projector-steered GAN synthesis, and (iii) quality-filtered augmentation, SEGA-NET achieves substantial improvements across five benchmarks (CIFAR-10/100, SVHN, Tiny-ImageNet, and Fashion-MNIST) and four architectures (ResNet, MobileNetV2, VGG-16, and ViT). Compared to conventional augmentations, SEGA-Net delivers $\boldsymbol{+} \mathbf{8} \boldsymbol{-} \mathbf{1 0 \%}$ gains in clean accuracy and adversarial robustness (FGSM, PGD, BIM, AutoAttack), while reducing generation cost by $6.7 \times$ relative to diffusion-based methods. These results demonstrate that semantic-guided synthesis with explicit quality control significantly enhances robustness and efficiency for resourceconstrained vision tasks.

title: Predictive Machine Learning Models for Optimizing the Financial Close Process
authors: Puneet Thakkar, Rahul Chawla
publish: 2026 International Conference on AI x Data and Knowledge Engineering (AIxDKE)
abstract: Within the financial organizations, financial close remains a process that consumes a lot of resources and missioncritical processes. However, performance on these affected by ongoing issues such as longer close cycles, reconciliation bottlenecks, rates of error, and gaps of compliance readiness, even with large investments in ERP systems and process automation. In order to maximize financial closing results in a variety of ways, this study suggests an integrated machine learning-based predictive analytics architecture. Four KPIsClose Cycle Time, Reconciliation Effort and compliance quality)-were employed to judge the quality of the work of regression models, such as linear regression, kernel-based Support Vector Regression, tree-based ensemble models, and neural network architectures. The top-performing model, which result in an R-Squared value of 0.9739 in error prediction and 0.8646 for days to close, and demonstrate the superiority are Multilayer Perceptrons with radial basis function, and Support Vector Regressors. Automation level, team experience, and manual journal entries are key predictive factors based on permutation-based feature significance analysis.

title: An Explainable Machine Learning Based Clinical Decision Support Framework for Early Heart Disease Risk Stratification
authors: Shubham Gupta, Suhaib Ahmed
publish: 2026 Innovations in Machine, Engineering, and Digital Conference (IMED)
abstract: Cardiovascular diseases are still the primary cause of mortality globally, and there is a necessity of credible and clear clinical decision support system to assist in risk identification at an early stage. Although machine learning methods have proved to be very strong predictors of heart diseases, their usage in clinical practice has been hindered by the lack of understanding and decision-oriented results. To overcome those issues, this paper describes an interpretable machine learning-driven clinical decision support system to risk-stratify patients with early heart disease by using structured clinical information. The framework incorporates Shapley Additive explanations (SHAP) with ensemble-based predictive modeling to give the right predictions and explanations of features (transparent). Various machine learning models (Logistic Regression, Support Vector Machine, Random Forest, and XGBoost) are tested based on standard performance measures. The experimental data indicate that the XGBoost model has very high performance with an accuracy of 93.2, precision of 93, recall of 92, F1-score of 92, and AUC of 0.96. Analysis of the confusion matrix shows that the false-negative rate is low, and this makes it applicable in clinical screening applications. Moreover, the risk stratification mechanism based on probability classifies patients into low risk, medium risk and high-risk groups, which allows making actionable clinical decisions beyond binary classification. The analysis done using global SHAP determines the clinical features, such as the type of chest pain, the highest heart rate reached, and exercise-induced angina, as important risk prediction factors. The findings indicate that the suggested framework is capable of balancing predictive performance, interpretability, and clinical usability to become one of the promising methods of reliable AI-assisted cardiovascular risk assessment.

title: Reliability Assessment of Early Exit Deep Convolutional Neural Networks
authors: Georgios Konstantinidis, Maria K. Michael, Theocharis Theocharides
abstract: Machine learning inference deployed on edge devices is subject to limited resources, which pushes for better energy and resource efficiency, while ensuring high performance. At the algorithmic level, dynamic Deep Neural Networks (dDNNs) have been proposed in an effort to improve performance and energy efficiency which is particularly useful in time-critical systems such as autonomous navigation and search and rescue operations. These systems are often deployed in harsh environments, making the hardware vulnerable to soft errors. This work investigates the reliability of such dDNNs, specifically convolutional, focusing on early exit approaches, in an effort to better comprehend the impact of the early exits on the overall reliability of dDNNs. We consider transient faults and use hardware-aware software fault models, combined with a fine-grained fault taxonomy, to derive failures-in-time (FIT) rate and assess reliability. We observe a new category of faults specific to dDNNs and we refer to them as non-materialized faults. In our assessment, we use a new fault taxonomy that is more fine-grained than existing ones, also including the new category of non-materialized faults. We explore various early exit scenarios, having as baseline the Google Inception V1 network deployed on an NVDLA accelerator to generate FIT rates and analyze the faults per network architecture block and operation type. Furthermore, we investigate the impact of faults on the overall performance (accuracy) and execution time (average inference time) of the neural network models. Based on our analysis we observe that the overall FIT rate remains similar among different early exit implementations (8.40-8.89), while the distribution of the critical faults is influenced by the network’s design-time decisions on early exit block placement and architecture.

title: Memory-Bounded A/RED: Scalable Active Detection of Rare Relevant Events in Indefinite Length Streams
authors: Rohan Loveland, Nathaniel Clark, Jason Gentile, Christopher Ritter
publish: 2026 International Conference on AI x Data and Knowledge Engineering (AIxDKE)
abstract: We present a memory-bounded, real-time Anomalous/Relevant Event Detection (A/RED) system for indefinitely long data streams. Extending prior work, our algorithm maintains bounded memory using a circular buffer of queried points and accelerates nearest-neighbor search via ball trees, reducing complexity from $O\left(n^{2}\right)$ to $O(\log n)$ per point. Small clusters are periodically merged to control model size, while a tunable paranoia parameter $\kappa$ regulates query rate. Evaluated on synthetic (NICE), projected (MNIST-2D), and high-dimensional (EMNIST) streams, A/RED achieves near-perfect relevant recall and query precision on well-separated data, and significantly outperforms random querying on challenging real-world distributions discovering rare relevant classes with minimal user burden. This work enables scalable, human-in-the-loop anomaly detection in continuous, high-volume sensor environments.

title: Experimental Study on Condensation Heat Transfer Enhancement under Non-Uniform Electric Fields_supp1-3685987.pdf
authors: Jian Wu
abstract: Condensation heat-transfer enhancement remains a key focus in thermal science and engineering. Conventional methods face limitations in efficiency, energy demand, and applicability, while non-uniform electric fields have recently shown distinctive advantages. Yet, previous work has often lacked clear visualization and systematic analysis of coupled influencing factors. In this study, a custom platform combining high-resolution imaging and multi-channel data acquisition was developed to investigate condensation under electric fields. The setup enables real-time observation of droplet dynamics together with synchronous heat-flux and temperature measurements across varied electrode structures, polarities, spacings, and voltages. Results reveal that enhancement mechanisms involve earlier droplet onset, accelerated droplet coalescence, and reduced detachment size. Negative corona generally outperforms positive corona under the tested conditions, although the magnitude of the polarity effect depends on electrode configuration and operating parameters. Moreover, under nominal average field strength, larger electrode spacing is positively correlated with enhancement. The optimal configuration (dual-needle electrodes, 10 mm spacing, +10 kV) yielded a maximum 32.78% improvement. This study provides a visualization-based and heat-flux-resolved experimental framework for understanding condensation under non-uniform electric fields and offers guidance for the design of compact electric-field-assisted condensation systems.

title: A Motion Control Algorithm of Wheel-Legged Robot via Adaptive Dynamic Programming and Data-Driven Value Iteration_supp1-3685946.mp4
authors: Aihong Ji
abstract: To enhance the motion adaptability of wheel legged robot in complex environments, a novel data-driven value iteration (VI) control algorithm based on adaptive dynamic programming (ADP) is proposed. A state-space system model of the wheel-legged robot is established, from which the algebraic Riccati equation containing the weighting matrices is derived based on the Hamilton-Jacobi-Bellman equation. The ADP framework is employed to collect state variable data under external disturbance, and an offline VI-based iteration learning process is conducted to obtain a set of optimal gain matrices. These gain matrices are subsequently integrated with multi-subtask motion control and torque allocation strategy to construct a unified control framework. Finally, a series of ex perimental evaluations, including dynamic balance, disturbance robustness, and stair-crossing, are conducted. The experimental results demonstrate that the proposed algorithm exhibits strong adaptability and superior disturbance robustness across complex and unstructured terrains.

title: Deep Learning-Based Process Control of Microrobot Swarms Guided by Phase Diagrams_supp4-3686187.mp4
authors: Jiangfan Yu
abstract: Microrobot swarms with locomotion dexterity and shape reconfigurability show immense potential in biomedical applications. Automatic control strategies are critical for the navigation of swarms in unstructured environments. Existing control methods mainly focus on initial and final states of swarms, and swarm process control designed to avoid undesired states throughout the control process is yet investigated. In this work, we develop a deep learning-based process control strategy for swarms guided by phase diagrams. Two deep neural networks are respectively built to model the swarm shape and kinematics. Control approaches based on precise swarm models are designed to automatically tune multiple swarm parameters. A phase diagram-based controller is proposed to guide the swarm reconfiguration while eliminating the coupling effects between swarm parameters. The swarm is enabled to accurately track predefined trajectories while performing continuous reconfiguration with desired states during the entire process. By integrating the process control of swarm pattern and locomotion, the swarm can dynamically adapt to constrained unstructured spaces and achieve robust collision avoidance.

title: Machine Learning–Driven Failure Prediction in Large-Scale Cloud Systems
authors: Rahul Amte
publish: 2026 Innovations in Machine, Engineering, and Digital Conference (IMED)
abstract: The dependability of large-scale distributed systems has become a vital economic and operational concern as the world increasingly relies on cloud computing infrastructure. By 2025, hyperscale data centers are adopting proactive predictive maintenance (PdM) driven by Machine Learning (ML). This research paper provides a thorough overview of the state-of-the-art methodologies for predicting failures in cloud environments, concentrating on advances established up to the beginning of 2025. The effectiveness of hybrid Deep Learning frameworks, specifically the 1DCNN-Transformer architecture, is investigated in detail, as it has proven highly capable of capturing both local and global trends in massive log streams. Furthermore, the application of dynamic feature selection algorithms, such as Wear-out-updating Ensemble Feature Ranking (WEFR), is explored within the scope of Solid-State Drive (SSD) failure prediction based on heterogeneous datasets. The report also examines the Federated Learning (FL) paradigm as a mechanism for cross-organizational cooperation while maintaining data privacy, highlighting adaptive structures that reduce communication overheads by up to 97%. Finally, it explores how these predictive models are incorporated into autonomous self-healing ecosystems, noting the financial implications of downtime and the ethical consequences of algorithmic bias in the age of automation.

title: Swarm Intelligence for Real-Time Traffic Flow Prediction in Urban Mobility Networks
authors: Shivendra Kumar Singh, Dharmendra Sharma, Saleh Naji Sreseh, Maki Mahdi Abdulhasan, Hassan Mohammed Abed, P. Gomathi
publish: 2026 Innovations in Machine, Engineering, and Digital Conference (IMED)
abstract: Owing to physical capacity limitations, unstable traffic flows, and increasing vehicle densities, metropolitan mobility networks are now subject to a great deal of stress. This study explores the potential for the development of an adaptive and dynamic model applied to real-time traffic flow prediction using swarm intelligence and specifically Particle Swarm Optimization (PSO) and Ant Colony Optimization (ACO). The idea is to use the characteristics of swarm intelligence, such as collective behavior and decentralized decision making, to build the proposed Swarm Intelligence-Based Traffic Predictor (SITP) that adjusts in real time to developing road conditions and traffic sensor data. Performance models were trained and validated using real traffic datasets obtained from urban traffic monitoring systems. The results of the study suggest improved prediction performance, improved convergence time, and improved adaptability over traditional machine learning techniques. The paper describes the system architecture, algorithm, simulation results, and comparison to demonstrate the potential of SITP for intelligent urban mobility management.

title: AI-Powered FinTech Analytics for Transactional Transparency and Fraud Mitigation in Industrial IoT Ecosystems
authors: Swetha Sistla, Mohan Sankaran, Nagaraju Jooluri, Kiran Veernapu, Priyameet Kaur Keer, Sonia Vaz
publish: 2026 Innovations in Machine, Engineering, and Digital Conference (IMED)
abstract: The intensive introduction of Industrial Internet of Things (IIoT) technologies has already increased the quantity and complexity of machine-to-machine financial transactions, which has become a significant challenge in terms of the clarity of the story and exclusion of threats of fraud. The idea proposed in this paper is AI-based FinTech analytics that feature both supervised and non-supervised machine learning systems when detecting fraud in industrial transactional environments. Advanced feature engineering and imbalance-sensitive model evaluation metrics are used to improve detection accuracy and reliability. Explainable artificial intelligence methods have been used to foster regulatory trust. The experimental findings show that there is a high rate of fraud detection, a low rate of false alarms, and applicability in real time, which validates the efficiency of the proposed approach in light of safe and transparent IIoT-based financial systems. The superior performance of XGBoost (97.6%) is attributed to the optimization of the gradient for fraud detection.

title: Policy-Aware Adaptive Data Processing Framework for Multi-Tenant Cloud Analytics Platforms
authors: Jitendra Gopaluni, Voolla Sandeep Kumar, Satvik Bhasin, Pushpanjali Chauhan
publish: 2026 Innovations in Machine, Engineering, and Digital Conference (IMED)
abstract: Multi-tenant cloud analytics systems have to be able to handle heterogeneous workloads effectively and observe tenant-specific policies, including service-level agreements, data locality, security and cost limits. Old methods of managing resources are not suitable to address the dynamic load and the policy requirements. This paper presents a Policy-Aware Adaptive Data Processing Framework (PA-ADPF), which combines machine learning algorithms to collaboratively achieve performance and policy fidelity in multi-tenant cloud systems. The framework applies supervised learning to predict the risk of policy violation, regression to approximate the number of resources needed, and reinforcement learning to allow adaptive decisions regarding scheduling. Empirical analysis of simulated multi-tenant workloads indicates that the proposed framework is highly effective to reduce the execution latency and policy violations and to enhance the throughput and resource utilization in comparison with the baseline approaches. The findings support the idea that integrating policy awareness into machine learning (ML)based decision-making allows scalable and efficient with average latency as 295 ms, throughput is 690 jobs per hour and resource utilization is 83%.
