publish: 2026 9th International Symposium on Big Data and Applied Statistics (ISBDAS)

title: Algorithm Design and Application in Enterprise Supply Chain Optimization
authors: Ting Yu
publish: 2025 International Conference on Artificial Intelligence, Electrical and Electronic Engineering (AI3E)
abstract: This article focuses on the optimization of enterprise supply chain, aiming at solving many problems faced by enterprises in supply chain management and improving the overall efficiency of supply chain through scientific algorithm design and application. Taking the manufacturing enterprise as an example, this article uses dynamic programming algorithm, improved GA (Genetic algorithm) and the combination of analytic hierarchy process and fuzzy comprehensive evaluation method to construct the algorithm models of inventory management, transportation scheduling and supplier selection respectively. The experimental verification and practical case analysis show that the inventory management model based on dynamic programming algorithm narrows the fluctuation range of inventory cost from 300,000 390,000 yuan in the traditional strategy to $230,000-280,000$ yuan. The improved GA transportation scheduling model can reduce the average transportation cost from 5282.5 yuan to 4125 yuan, and shorten the delivery time from 5.35 days to 4.1 days. Based on the combination of AHP and fuzzy comprehensive evaluation model, the comprehensive index of cooperation performance remains at 85-91, which is much higher than 70-78 for empirical suppliers. It can be seen that the algorithm model has achieved remarkable results in improving the efficiency of enterprise supply chain management and reducing costs, and provided reliable decision support for the actual operation of enterprises.

title: Differential Privacy-Preserving Personalized Federated Learning via MetaLearning
authors: Guixin Wang, Yukun Zheng, Zhiming Cai
publish: 2025 International Conference on Artificial Intelligence, Electrical and Electronic Engineering (AI3E)
abstract: To effectively protect user data privacy and realize distributed personalized training in edge computing scenarios, a personalized federated learning method based on differential privacy is proposed. A personalized federated learning framework was built that allowed participating clients to train models using local data and apply differential privacy techniques, introducing Gaussian noise that met local differential privacy requirements to protect data privacy. The client uploads the model parameters with differential privacy protection to the server, which aggregates and updates the global model before distributing it to the client for the next round of updates. This process is repeated until the model meets the differential privacy requirements. By integrating differential privacy mechanisms with personalized federated learning, all clients can jointly train a global meta model and adaptively adjust it based on their respective privacy datasets to generate personalized models that conform to local data characteristics, ensuring the utility and communication efficiency of personalized models while ensuring privacy and security of the data. The experimental results show that the method can achieve a balance between data privacy and model performance for different privacy budgets. At the same time, compared with other methods, this method shows significant advantages in model convergence accuracy, training time, etc., and can maintain a stable classification accuracy of over 90% for clothing images while ensuring data privacy and security, significantly improving the efficiency and model performance of distributed personalized training.

title: SIAgent: Spatial Interaction Agent Via LLM-Powered Eye-Hand Motion Intent Understanding in VR_supp1-3686395.mp4
authors: Feng Lu
abstract: Eye-hand coordinated interaction is becoming a mainstream interaction modality in Virtual Reality (VR) user interfaces. Current paradigms for this multimodal interaction require users to learn predefined gestures and memorize multiple gesture-task associations, which can be summarized as an “Operation-to-Intent” paradigm. This paradigm increases users' learning costs and has low interaction error tolerance. In this paper, we propose SIAgent, a novel “Intent-to-Operation” framework allowing users to express interaction intents through natural eye-hand motions based on common sense and habits. Our system features two main components: (1) intent recognition that translates spatial interaction data into natural language and infers user intent, and (2) agent-based execution that generates an agent to execute corresponding tasks. This eliminates the need for gesture memorization and accommodates individual motion preferences with high error tolerance. We conduct two user studies across over 60 interaction tasks, comparing our method with two “Operation-to-Intent” techniques. Results show our method achieves higher intent recognition accuracy than gaze + pinch interaction (97.2% vs 93.1%) while reducing arm fatigue and improving usability, and user preference. Another study verifies the function of eye gaze and hand motion channels in intent recognition. Our work offers valuable insights into enhancing VR interaction intelligence through intent-driven design. Our source code and LLM prompts will be made available upon publication. Our project page is at https://zhimin-wang.github.io/SIAgent.html

title: An Explainable GNN-Based Framework for Short-Term Bus Passenger Flow Forecasting
authors: A. R. Sana Ullah, Asiye Baghbani, Nizar Bouguila, Zachary Patterson
abstract: Accurate short-term passenger flow forecasting in bus networks is vital for optimizing transit operations and improving service reliability. However, due to complex spatial-temporal dependencies and dynamic demand patterns, this task remains challenging. While several deep learning models have been applied to passenger flow prediction, the increasing availability of real-time data has led to the growing use of graph neural networks (GNNs) combined with temporal models that effectively capture spatial and temporal dependencies by modeling transit networks as graphs of nodes and links. Yet, like many deep learning models, GNN-based approaches suffer from limited explainability, restricting their practical use. To address this challenge, we propose BNConv-LSTM—an explainable spatiotemporal graph-based framework for forecasting short-term bus passenger flow. Our proposed model captures spatial and temporal dependencies on the bus network by integrating a graph convolutional LSTM architecture with learnable spatial and temporal soft masks. These masks help improve the interpretability of the model’s predictions by highlighting the most influential bus stops and time steps contributing to each forecast. Evaluated on real-world data from the Ames Bus Network in Iowa, USA, BNConv-LSTM consistently demonstrates reliable performance in both prediction and explainability. The model’s learnable spatial and temporal soft masks offer interpretable insights into the spatial and temporal patterns influencing passenger demand, making it a powerful tool for both effective forecasting and well-informed transit planning.

title: Efficient Uncertainty Propagation in Continuous Bayesian Networks Using GUM and Monte Carlo Methods
authors: Luisa Hoffmann, Michael Heizmann, Ali Darijani, Jürgen Beyerer
abstract: In large and complex systems such as the Circular Factory, vast amounts of heterogeneous and uncertain data are generated. Classical Bayesian Networks (BNs) provide a principled framework for reasoning under uncertainty, but their reliance on discrete Conditional Probability Tables (CPTs) limits their applicability in continuous domains. In the context of semantic computing, where heterogeneous sensor data and knowledge graph information must be integrated, this limitation prevents probabilistic reasoning from being consistently applied to continuous quantities and measurement-based information. This paper introduces a framework for continuous Bayesian Networks that replaces discrete CPTs with functional dependencies and integrates the Guide to the Expression of Uncertainty in Measurement (GUM) as a standardized approach for analytical uncertainty propagation. The proposed method enables probabilistic reasoning over continuous variables while explicitly accounting for measurement and parameter uncertainty in a mathematically transparent way. To complement the analytical formulation, Monte Carlo (MC) simulations with Gaussian Mixture Models (GMMs) are employed to handle non-linear and non-Gaussian cases, allowing for numerical validation of the GUM approximation. We present illustrative examples comparing both approaches and discuss their agreement and limitations. The results demonstrate that GUM-based inference provides accurate and computationally efficient uncertainty estimates in locally linear regions, while Monte Carlo methods serve as a reliable benchmark for more complex, non-linear dependencies. This framework thus lays the foundation for robust and interpretable uncertainty reasoning in environments such as the Circular Factory. Furthermore, it provides a bridge between probabilistic reasoning and semantic representations by enabling continuous uncertainty information to be embedded within knowledge-based systems, thus advancing uncertainty-aware semantic computing.

title: Research on Cross Mechanisms and Optimization Methods Based on Deep Reinforcement Learning and Multi-Task Learning
authors: Wenjia Jiang, Zhuang Bai, Xinsheng Shi
publish: 2025 International Conference on Artificial Intelligence, Electrical and Electronic Engineering (AI3E)
abstract: With the rapid development of artificial intelligence, the integration of deep reinforcement learning (DRL) and multi-task learning (MTL) has emerged as a key research paradigm for enhancing agents’ generalization ability and learning efficiency. This paper systematically reviews recent advancements in this field, focusing on core methodologies and representative applications. We discuss how MTL frameworks address fundamental challenges in DRL, such as low sample efficiency and limited generalization, by enabling effective knowledge sharing across multiple tasks. Key techniques, including shared representations, policy distillation, and metalearning, are analyzed together with their formal mathematical formulations. Experimental results demonstrate the effectiveness of these approaches in improving agent performance. Finally, current limitations and promising future research directions are highlighted, including more efficient knowledge transfer, dynamic task structuring, and secure MTL.

title: Teacher Emotion Perception and Adaptive Regulation Based on ARTEMIS Artwork Annotations
authors: Wu Wang, Qiang Wan
publish: 2026 International Conference on Blockchain Technology and Foundation Models (BTFM)
abstract: This study proposes a media-based interactive emotion perception and adaptive regulation framework for teachers based on emotion annotations from the ARTEMIS dataset. Discrete emotion categories are mapped into a continuous valence-arousal (V-A) space following the Russell circumplex model. A dynamic target emotion is defined as a moderately positive and low-arousal state (v=0.5, a=0.4). Teachers’ workday emotion dynamics (08:00–16:00) are modeled using a time-series approach, where morning elevation and sin(π(t−8)/4) and −sin(π(t−12)/4), respectively. The Euclidean distance between the current emotional state and the target is used to quantify regulation demand, and an adaptive strategy is generated via a sliding window algorithm (window size = 5). Emotion density analysis and stability metrics are further employed to identify optimal and critical intervention moments, based on which personalized artwork recommendation sequences are produced to support indirect emotion regulation for teachers.
